analogy: Think of "Election and Representation" through the chapter's own opening images: 51 Chapter 3: Election and Representation Chapter Three ELECTION AND REPRESENTATION INTRODUCTION Have you ever played chess? What would happen if the black knight suddenly started moving straight rather than two and a half squares? Or, what would happen if in a game of cricket,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: ELECTION AND, REPRESENTATION, INTRODUCTION, ELECTIONS AND DEMOCRACY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Election and Representation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 51 Chapter 3: Election and Representation Chapter Three ELECTION AND REPRESENTATION INTRODUCTION Have you ever played chess?

The text explains that what would happen if the black knight suddenly started moving straight rather than two and a half squares?

The text explains that or, what would happen if in a game of cricket, there were no umpires?

The text explains that in any sport, we need to follow certain rules.

The text explains that change the rules and the outcome of the game would be very different.

The text explains that similarly a game needs an impartial umpire whose decision is accepted by all the players.

The text explains that the rules and the umpire have to be agreed upon before we begin to play a game.

The text explains that what is true of a game is also true of elections.

The text explains that there are different rules or systems of conducting elections.

The text explains that the outcome of the election depends on the rules we have adopted.

The text explains that we need some machinery to conduct the elections in an impartial manner.

The text explains that since these two decisions need to be taken before the game of electoral politics can begin, these cannot be left to any government.

The text explains that that is why these basic decisions about elections are written down in the constitution of a democratic country.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall study the constitutional provisions regarding elections and representation.

The text explains that we shall focus on the importance of the method of election chosen in our Constitution and the implications of the constitutional provisions regarding impartial machinery for conducting elections.

The text explains that we shall also look at some suggestions for amending the constitutional provisions in this respect.

The text explains that after reading this chapter, you would understand: ± different methods of election; ± the characteristics of the system of election adopted in our country; ± the importance of the provisions for free and fair elections; and ± the debate on electoral reforms.

The text explains that 52 Indian Constitution at Work ELECTIONS AND DEMOCRACY Let us begin by asking ourselves two simple questions about elections and democracy. ± Can we have democracy without holding elections? ± Can we hold elections without having democracy?

The text explains that let us have a discussion in the classroom on both these questions by using examples from whatever we have learnt so far in the previous classes.

The text explains that the first question reminds us of the necessity of representation in a large democracy.

The text explains that all citizens cannot take direct part in making every decision.

The text explains that elections have today become the most visible symbol of the democratic process.

The text explains that we often distinguish between direct and indirect democracy.

The text explains that a direct democracy is one where the citizens directly participate in the day-to-day decision- making and in the running of the government.

The text explains that the ancient city-states in Greece were considered examples of direct democracy.

The text explains that many would consider local governments, especially gram sabhas, to be the closest examples of direct They say elections are carnival of democracy.

The text explains that 53 Chapter 3: Election and Representation democracy.

The text explains that but this kind of direct democracy cannot be practiced when a decision has to be taken by lakhs and crores of people.

The text explains that citizens are involved only indirectly, through their elected representatives.

The text explains that the second question reminds us of the fact that not all elections are democratic.

The text explains that the constitution of a democratic country lays down some basic rules about elections.

The text explains that what is the need of writing these rules in the constitution?

The text explains that 54 Indian Constitution at Work Like most democratic constitutions, the Constitution of India answers all these questions.

The text explains that as you can see, the first three questions are about ensuring that elections are free and fair and can thus be called democratic.

The text explains that the last two questions are about ensuring a fair representation.

The text explains that in this chapter you will consider both these aspects of the Constitutional provisions about elections.

The text explains that eLECTION SYSTEM IN INDIA You may have noted above a reference to different methods or the systems of elections.

The text explains that you may have wondered why the constitution needs to write down how the votes are to be counted and representatives elected.

The text explains that we need to, because this question is not as simple as it appears to us.

The text explains that we have got so used to our system of elections that we think that there cannot be any other way.

The text explains that 55 Chapter 3: Election and Representation can be very different ways in which people make their choices and very different ways in which their preferences can be counted.

The text explains that first Past the Post System Look at the newspaper clipping.

The text explains that 56 Indian Constitution at Work It talks of one historic moment in India’s democracy.

The text explains that how could our Constitution makers accept such an unfair system?

The text explains that under this system: ± The entire country is divided into 543 constituencies; ± Each constituency elects one representative; and ± The candidate who secures the highest number of votes in that constituency is declared elected.

The text explains that there should be no discrepancy between votes and seats for different parties. d.

The text explains that people should be able to elect a good candidate even if they do not like his or her political party.

The text explains that a former Chief Election Commissioner joined a political party and contested elections.

The text explains that one view is that a former Election Commissioner is an independent citizen and has a right to join any political party and to contest election.

The text explains that according to the other view, leaving this possibility open

The text explains that 77 Chapter 3: Election and Representation can affect the impartiality of the Election Commission.

The text explains that so, former Election Commissioners must not be allowed to contest any elections.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work, Chapter 3: Election and Representation

revision_bullets: 51 Chapter 3: Election and Representation Chapter Three ELECTION AND REPRESENTATION INTRODUCTION Have you ever played chess? (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
What would happen if the black knight suddenly started moving straight rather than two and a half squares? (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
Or, what would happen if in a game of cricket, there were no umpires? (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
Change the rules and the outcome of the game would be very different. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
Similarly a game needs an impartial umpire whose decision is accepted by all the players. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
The rules and the umpire have to be agreed upon before we begin to play a game. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
There are different rules or systems of conducting elections. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
The outcome of the election depends on the rules we have adopted. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
We need some machinery to conduct the elections in an impartial manner. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)
Since these two decisions need to be taken before the game of electoral politics can begin, these cannot be left to any government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: This
schemes.point: (A party must get a minimum of 3.25% votes to be eligible to get seats in the legislature.) This often leads to a multi-party coalition government. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 7)

schemes.name: More
schemes.point: More importantly, the makers of our Constitution also felt that PR based election may not be suitable for giving a stable government in a parliamentary system. (NCERT Class 11 Indian Constitution at Work Ch3 pg 10)

prelims_traps: Do not treat ELECTION AND and REPRESENTATION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Election and Representation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Election and Representation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ELECTION AND and REPRESENTATION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
